# 1. Introduction

Natural resource extraction and agriculture occupy an uneasy but important place in many African economies. Minerals, oil, gas, and precious metals generate public revenue, foreign exchange, employment, and infrastructure investment [@afdb2016; @cust2023; @harding2016; @imf2015; @mabe2021];[^1]. Agriculture, meanwhile, remains central to food security, rural livelihoods, and poverty reduction [@fao2023; @gollin2010; @fao2023a; @mwaniki2006; @braun2023; @change2016; @macpherson2022; @christiaensen2011; @collier2014]. The challenge is that these sectors often depend on overlapping land and water resources. Mineral mining, quarrying, and sand winning may expand economic opportunities, but they can also undermine agricultural productivity by degrading land, polluting water resources, altering local labour markets, contaminating soils, and contributing to deforestation [@chopra2022; @dorinet2021; @abutu2022; @aragn2016; @awuah2023; @unep2023; @ouoba2018; @worlanyo2021; @bravoortega2007; @ebeke2015; @kotsadam2016; @li2015].

The relationship between extraction and agriculture is therefore unlikely to be uniform. Resource revenues may finance infrastructure and agricultural investment [@aragn2015; @buur2020; @cust2014; @frederiksen2019] but extractive activities can also encroach on farmland, reduce land tenure security, disrupt access to agricultural inputs and services, and disincentivize farmers' investments in productivity-enhancing technologies. These effects matter because productivity differences among farmers may arise from two distinct sources. Farmers may be inefficient in using the technology available to them, or they may operate with a lower production technology set in the first place. Thus, typical policy responses aimed only at improving technical efficiency will not fully address a problem rooted in land degradation, insecure farmland access, or weak technology availability.

Ghana provides a useful setting for examining this issue. The country has long depended on gold, other minerals, and more recently, oil and gas to finance government budget while agriculture remains a major contributor to GDP and national food security [@worldbank2011; @gyampo2010; @mihalyi2021; @oforiadofo2022; @fosu2017; @mabe2023; @bansah2016; @ennin2023]. Mineral mining has attracted foreign direct investments and contributed to public revenues, but it has also raised concerns about environmental degradation, land-use competition, and livelihood disruption [@shiquan2022; @sun2021; @xiong2023; @keay2015]. These concerns are especially visible in the artisanal and small-scale mining sector (locally known as "galamsey"), where mining activities often overlap with cocoa and food crop areas. Recent evidence from Ghana shows that farmland acquisition for galamsey can generate pressure on farmers and landowners, including "coerced to sell" arrangements in cocoa-growing communities that reduce food security [@dorinet2021; @nunoo2023]. More broadly, studies of Ghana's mining sector point to long-standing land-use competition between artisanal and licensed commercial miners and a policy environment that has often favored large-scale mineral development over local livelihood concerns [@hilson2019; @hilson2002].

Existing studies provide important evidence on the agricultural effects of natural resources and mining. At the macro level, extractive resources have been linked to structural transformation challenges, resource dependence, and possible crowding out of agriculture. Meanwhile, studies at the local level show that mining can have diverse effects on agricultural productivity, food security, land access, and environmental quality. However, most existing work does not distinguish between lower farm performance caused by weak technology access and that caused by inefficient use of available technology. This distinction matters for policy. A technology gap points to constraints in the production environment, input systems, land access, or agricultural investment. A technical efficiency gap points more directly to farm management, information, or input-use decisions.

This paper examines how extractive land-use activities are associated with crop production technology and efficiency in Ghana. We focus on three broad classes of extraction: gold and bauxite mining, sand winning, and stone quarrying. These activities differ in their land requirements, environmental effects, regulatory arrangements, and links to local labour and input markets. Treating them as a single issue may therefore conceal important differences in how extraction affects agricultural production. We address three questions. First, do farmers in extraction communities operate with different production technologies from farmers in non-extraction communities? Second, do observed differences arise from technology gaps, technical inefficiency, or both? Third, do these relationships vary across extraction activities, survey rounds, farmer characteristics, crops, and regions?

To answer these questions, we combine four waves of the Ghana Living Standards Survey covering 26,811 crop farmers with community-level information on extraction activities. We use a meta-stochastic frontier framework to decompose farm performance into the Technology Gap Ratio (TGR), Technical Efficiency (TE), and Meta-frontier Technical Efficiency (MTE). The TGR captures the productive capacity of the technology available to a group; TE captures how efficiently farmers use that technology; and MTE measures performance relative to a common meta-frontier. We also use matching to improve comparability between farmers in extraction and non-extraction communities. This approach builds on the meta-frontier literature showing that producers operating under different technologies should not be assessed against a single production frontier, and on Ghanaian evidence that technology and managerial performance vary across farmer groups,.

The results show that extraction is associated with a modest but statistically significant technology gap rather than with less capable farming. Farmers in extraction communities operate with weaker production technology than comparable farmers elsewhere, yet use the technology available to them more efficiently; because the two margins nearly offset, their overall performance is only slightly lower. The more policy-relevant patterns emerge when extraction is disaggregated: mineral mining and quarrying are associated with lower performance mainly through technology gaps, informal or small-scale mining with both weaker technology access and lower efficiency, and sand winning with weaker technology but substantially higher efficiency. Section 5 reports the estimates.

The paper contributes to the literature in three ways. First, it adds farm-level evidence to debates on resource extraction and agricultural development by showing that extraction affects crop production through different technology and efficiency channels. Second, it contributes to land-use policy debates by demonstrating that mining, quarrying, and sand winning should not be treated as homogeneous land-use pressures. Different extraction activities imply different constraints and therefore different policy responses. Third, it extends the application of meta-frontier methods in agricultural economics to a land-use conflict setting. By separating technology gaps from technical efficiency, the paper shows why policies focused only on farmer management may be insufficient where the central constraint is the production technology available in extraction-affected communities.

The rest of the paper is organized as follows. The next section presents details of the data. It first discusses how we construct extraction and non-extraction communities from the four GLSS rounds. This is followed by an exploration of the descriptive statistics on farmers in the sample. Section 3 contextualizes the state of natural resource extraction in Ghana. It begins by discussing the prevalence of the three extraction classes in crop-producing communities in Ghana, and ties this to recent land-use policies at the intersection of agricultural modernization and resource marketing and governance. Section 4 highlights our empirical specification of the meta-stochastic frontier model and the matching strategy. We discuss results on model diagnostics, output elasticities, (dis-)aggregated efficiency estimates, and robustness checks on different model specifications in Section 5. Finally, Section 6 concludes the paper and suggests how Ghanaian policymakers could design land-use policies to achieve win-win economic development and environmental management outcomes.
